title: Practical Entanglement Distillation Protocols with Quadratic Error Suppression
abstract: Near-term and early fault-tolerant quantum computing architectures are expected to exhibit highly non-uniform error rates. In particular, local operations within a chip can be substantially more reliable than operations connecting different chips or dilution refrigerators. Such inter-module operations can therefore become a dominant bottleneck, even when quantum error correction is applied. Entanglement distillation provides a natural way to trade additional operations and qubits for higher-fidelity entanglement. Standard distillation protocols, however, are usually formulated in an LOCC resource model, in which several noisy Bell pairs are generated initially and all subsequent processing consists only of local operations and classical communication. Here, we consider a generalized model tailored to modular quantum computing hardware, in which the two modules have access to high-fidelity local operations and to repeated uses of the same noisy inter-module entangling operation during the protocol. We develop practical small-scale entanglement distillation protocols designed to minimize both space and time overhead. Remarkably, our main protocol requires only two qubits per module, yet achieves quadratic error suppression of inter-module errors, assuming local operations are much cleaner. Compared with existing small-scale protocols, our space-optimal protocol provides more space- and time-efficient quadratic error suppression and achieves the best performance in our simulations and experiments on noisy links of current superconducting quantum processors. These results suggest that inter-module-gate-assisted entanglement distillation can be a practical primitive for overcoming noisy links in modular quantum computing architectures.
summary: This paper introduces new, highly efficient entanglement distillation protocols designed for modular quantum computing. By using inter-module gates as active components, the proposed protocols achieve quadratic error suppression using only two qubits per module, making them practical for near-term hardware.
main_problem: Inter-module operations in modular quantum computing architectures exhibit much higher error rates than local operations, creating a bottleneck for scaling. Existing entanglement distillation protocols are often too resource-intensive in terms of qubits and time for near-term hardware.
main_result: The authors developed a space-optimal distillation protocol requiring only two qubits per module that achieves quadratic error suppression of inter-module errors. This protocol outperforms existing small-scale protocols in both simulations and experiments on superconducting hardware.
method: The study uses a generalized resource model where inter-module entangling operations are used actively during the protocol. The researchers compared new protocols against established ones using numerical simulations and experimental implementation on the IBM pittsburgh processor.
model_or_system: The system is a modular quantum computing architecture, specifically implemented and tested on the IBM pittsburgh superconducting quantum processor. The noise model uses a stochastic Pauli channel with single-qubit and two-qubit correlated errors.
key_observables: Bell-state fidelity, normalized error, output error rate, and protocol duration (depth and wall-clock time).
important_parameters: Inter-module error rate (epsilon_inter), local error rates, qubit count per module, and gate durations (e.g., 88 ns for CZ, 32 ns for single-qubit gates).
main_assumptions: Local operations and measurements are assumed to be significantly more reliable than inter-module operations; the low-error regime is the primary focus.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows existing recurrence and large-scale protocols; Figure 2 illustrates the new space-optimal and high-rate protocols; Figure 3 shows simulations of output error vs. inter-module error; Figure 4 presents experimental results comparing error suppression and protocol duration on IBM hardware; Figure 5 shows the device layout and hardware noise statistics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the bottleneck of inter-module errors, proposes new space-efficient distillation protocols, provides analytical error suppression analysis, compares these protocols to existing methods via simulation, and validates the results through experiments on a superconducting processor.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Existing protocols. Blue operations are local, red operations are noisy inter-module operations. The protocol accepts if c1 = c2, c3 = c4 and c5 = c6. (a)-(c) Single recurrence checks in the Z, X and Y bases, each detecting two of the three Pauli error types. (d) Two recurrence rounds (here Z and X) detect any single error. (e) Maneva-Smolin protocol, which increases the output rate by checking multiple Bell pairs jointly. (f) Leung-Shor protocol, which outputs two Bell pairs with quadratic error suppression.

FIG. 2: Our new efficient protocols. Blue operations are local, red operations are noisy inter-module operations. The protocol accepts if c1 = c2 and c3 = c4, and in (a) additionally requires c3 = c4 = 0. (a) Space-optimal protocol with quadratic suppression of any two-qubit Pauli errors. (b) Reduced space-optimal protocol with fewer gates, achieving quadratic suppression only for arbitrary single-qubit Pauli errors. (c) High-rate protocol acting on m qubits per module and outputting m −2 Bell pairs with quadratic error suppression.

FIG. 3: Simulations comparing the output error of the different protocols for varying inter-module error εinter. The depolarizing error of the local operations increases from 10−8 in (a) and (b) to 10−6 in (c) and (d) and to 10−4 in (e) and (f). In (a), (c) and (e) we consider only single-qubit depolarizing errors, while in (b), (d) and (f) we consider uniform two-qubit depolarizing errors.

FIG. 4: Experimental results comparing the different protocols. (a) The output error for varying raw CZ errors. (b) The total and normalized output error for the multi-output protocols as a function of the number of Bell pairs they produce. (c) The total and normalized duration of the protocols when compiled on ibm pittsburgh as a function of the number of Bell pairs that are produced. Note that the single-recurrence protocol corresponds to the Maneva-Smolin protocol with one output Bell pair and similarly the Leung-Shor protocol corresponds to the high-rate protocol with two output Bell pairs.

FIG. 5: Implementation details of the experiments. (a) The device layout of ibm pittsburgh, with the three noisy links used in the experiments indicated by a red box. (b) The cumulative distribution of the T1 and T2 coherence times and (c) of the single qubit gate (SX), readout (Meas.) and two-qubit gate (CZ) error rates of the qubits used in the experiments, as well as the corresponding median values.
